projects.title: Frankquency 1.0
projects.description: A personally curated radio & podcast streaming app

summary.heading: Context:
summary.content: I wanted an easy way to play my favorite podcasts and radio stations from around the world without having to install an app from an appstore or pay for a subscription.

summary.heading: Process:
summary.content: I built a Node.js app and configured it on my home server, allowing any client device on my network to stream my curated list of sound streams.

summary.heading: Outcome
summary.content: A lightweight streaming solution that eliminates daily friction and cognitive load by consolidating multiple audio streaming sources into a unified, easy-to-use interface. By hosting the service locally, I can stream global radio and podcast feeds from any device. No more context switching between this podcast app and that music station.

summary.heading: Learnings
summary.content: I learned about the podcast XML format, how RSS feeds work, and local networking configuration. I also experimented with Claude Code for this project to get a better understanding of current AI developer workflows and related UX patterns. I learned to plan up front with plan mode, ask clarifying questions to iterate on that plan and understand the technical approach, before implementing the changes.

summary.heading: Next steps
summary.content: Build an authenticated admin panel front-end to manage the list of saved podcasts / radio stations without having to go into the code. Containerize it with Docker. Add a database for 'Recently Played' or 'Favorites'.

projects.title: Personal Weather App 1.0
projects.description: A simple web app that fetches live forecast data and presents it in a dashboard-like interface

summary.heading: Context
summary.content: During fire season, I wanted to know the air quality for the region to avoid health issues while hiking. I also wanted to have an answer to the common questions, "What should I wear? Do you think its going to rain today?"

summary.heading: Process
summary.content: I built a webpage with HTML/CSS/JS to display the current weather conditions, hourly and daily forecasts, air charts for data points including Air Quality Index (AQI). It leverages weather.gov and Open-Meteo APIs and parses the JSON responses and render the forecast information in a digestable, easy-to-read interface.

summary.heading: Outcome
summary.content: I pinned the weather dashboard in my browser tabs so the current forecast is a click away when I want to know what the weather is like.

summary.heading: Learnings
summary.content: I enjoyed digging into the documentation for the chart.js implementation. I wanted to display some charts to better visualize the forecasts so this was a nice way to expand my Javascript capabilties.

summary.heading: Next steps
summary.content: Revisit the layout and CSS. Incorporate more charts but style them consistently with the rest of the interface.

projects.title: Aircraft Tracker 1.0
projects.description: Leverages APIs to fetch data about what aircrafts are flying overhead and presents it in an easy-to-use map interface

summary.heading: Context:
summary.content: We live within 15 minutes of a busy international airport and other small regional airfields, so we frequently hear the sound of overhead air traffic. I'm naturally curious and wanted a quick way to identify the aircraft flying over my house, where it's coming from and where it's headed. I built a React app that fetches live aircraft data and displays them on an interactive map with details like altitude, heading, and velocity.

summary.heading: Process:
summary.content: This was one of my first experiments with React, so I did lean more heavily on Claude to help wire up the app. I used webpack and npm scripts for the build setup. The app fetches live data from the OpenSky API and renders them on a MapLibre GL map. I also leveraged the callsign returned from the API to construct a FlightAware URL, giving users a clickable link to more flight details. Authentication with OpenSky is handled via OAuth using a clientID and secret, which are stored in a .env file.

summary.heading: Outcome
summary.content: I know have a React app that runs on my local network, so it is accessible from both my desktop and phone. Now, when I hear a plane fly over head, I can quickly learn all about it.

summary.heading: Learnings
summary.content: This project deepened my understanding of React, webpack, and npm scripts. I also got hands-on experience with MapLibre GL for map rendering, DOM manipulation and OAuth authentication flows.

summary.heading: Next steps
summary.content: Overlay local airport boundaries on the map so users can distinguish between aircraft flying overhead from those taxiing on the runway. I'd also like to explore a lookup feature that allows users to track a specific inbound flight and estimate the ideal time to depart the house for airport pickups.

projects.title: Configured Pi-hole
projects.description: I set up a home network-wide ad blocker for (mostly) ad-free browsing on all devices

summary.heading: Context
summary.content: I love to cook, but I absolutely hate recipe websites with all of the ads. I wanted a way to easily browse recipes and websites without constantly having to dismiss ads.

summary.heading: Process
summary.content: I used a Raspberry Pi and configured Pi-hole, a network-wide ad blocker, that acts as a Domain Name System-sinkhole and protects all of your network devices from unwanted ad content without having to install any client-side software.

summary.heading: Outcome
summary.content: As a result, over 27% of queries have been blocked on our network since configuring the pi-hole. Network performance has improved due to the fact that ads are blocked before they are even downloaded.

summary.heading: Learnings
summary.content: I learned more about the Domain Name System (DNS) and how our devices depend on it. I also got more comfortable making changes in my router's admin panel, such as assigning static IPs to specific devices using Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol (DHCP). Finally, I learned the importance of redundancy because the moment my Pi goes down, our whole internet will stop working!

summary.heading: Next steps
summary.content: Purchase another Raspberry Pi and setup pi-hole to that we have a back-up in place if the main pi-hole goes down.

projects.title: Random User UX Widget Library
projects.description: A Javascript-powered prototyping widget library for rapid prototyping in Axure RP

summary.heading: Context
summary.content: The Axure RP desktop application does not provide users with a way to easily insert randomly-generated content from the web. I wanted an easy way to prototype with random content, without having to write out my own random text strings or find image sources online.

summary.heading: Process
summary.content: I created a library of widgets that leverage Javascript and make a call to the Random User Generator API to add random user avatars, names, addresses, etc. to the prototype at runtime.

summary.heading: Outcome
summary.content: As a result, I can now quickly create prototypes with random content without having to search every time.

summary.heading: Learnings
summary.content: I gained a better understanding of how to use query selectors to update DOM elements.

summary.heading: Next steps
summary.content: Perhaps I'll rebuild the library as actual stand-alone web components that I can leverage in my future coding projects. Stay tuned for more!

projects.title: Plant Something Prototype
projects.description: A fully functional UX prototype that helps users grow healthy plants from seed

summary.heading: Context
summary.content: I like to grow plants from seeds and wanted a way to quickly access information such as planting depth, ideal soil temperature, and spacing and nutrient requirements.

summary.heading: Process
summary.content: I created a Google Sheet with data for each seed purchased. I then imported the data into Axure RP, a UX tool for building realistic prototypes. The Repeater widget and Dynamic Panels were used to display the data for each seed. The prototype allows users to browse the seed catalog or search by seed name. I published the working prototype to Axure Cloud and added a shortcut on my cell phone to quickly access my prototype whether I was on the go or in the garden.

summary.heading: Outcome
summary.content: As a result, I can now quickly pull up key information about my plants throughout the growing season without having to stop what I'm doing and consult my monstrosity of a spreadsheet.

summary.heading: Learnings
summary.content: One thing I learned is how to structure my projects more cleanly by maintaining clear separation between the data and the presentation layers. This is challenging to do with prototyping software that doesn't connect to a back-end database, but my approach helped me understand and appreciate the concerns that developers can experience when planning an application. I also learned the importance of backing up your work. Since I lost access to the original, locally-stored prototype file, I can no longer go back and make updates to the project. Let this be a reminder to back up your work!

summary.heading: Next steps
summary.content: With more knowledge of front-end development, I plan to move from a static spreadsheet+prototype to a full-stack Node.js application.
